content_markdown: A $1.0\text{ kW}$ heater supplies energy to a liquid of mass $0.50\text{ kg}$. The temperature of the liquid changes by $80\text{ K}$ in a time of $200\text{ s}$. The specific heat capacity of the liquid is $4.0\text{ kJ kg}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$. What is the average power lost by the liquid?
vibe_explanation: First, calculate the net power used to heat the liquid using $P_{net} = \frac{mc\Delta T}{t}$. Here, $P_{net} = \frac{0.50 \times 4000 \times 80}{200} = \frac{160000}{200} = 800\text{ W}$. The heater supplies $1000\text{ W}$ ($1.0\text{ kW}$), but only $800\text{ W}$ is actually increasing the liquid's temperature. Therefore, the power lost to the surroundings is $1000\text{ W} - 800\text{ W} = 200\text{ W}$.
